coredata.dc:title: DFT-based investigation of graphene-mediated manipulation for efficient potassium ion entanglement
coredata.dc:creator: Colherinhas, Guilherme
Cardoso, Wesley B.
coredata.dc:description:
In this paper we address the potential entanglement of potassium ions mediated by interaction with a graphene sheet using DFT. The system consists of a neutral graphene sheet (
G Q D
) positioned equidistant between two positively charged potassium ions (
K
+
). The entanglement is strongly influenced by the
K
+
@ G Q D @
K
+
separation distance, in which it is possible to observe specific electronic transitions originating exclusively from the GQD to the potassium ions, enabling the acquisition of entangled states between the two ions with fidelity close to unity, demonstrating the potential of the present system for a robust generation of quantum entanglement between alkali metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum entanglement
Graphene quantum dots
GQD
Density functional theory
Electronic transitions

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico
Instituto Nacional de Ciência e Tecnologia para Excitotoxicidade e Neuroproteção
Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior
Instituto Nacional de Ciencia e Tecnologia de Informacao Quantica

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the financial support of the Brazilian agencies CNPq (WBC: #306105/2022-5; GC: #304903/2020-5) and CAPES . This work was also performed as part of the Brazilian National Institute of Science and Technology (INCT) for Quantum Information (#465469/2014-0).

The authors would like to thank the financial support of the Brazilian agencies CNPq (WBC: #306105/2022-5; GC: #304903/2020-5) and CAPES. This work was also performed as part of the Brazilian National Institute of Science and Technology (INCT) for Quantum Information (#465469/2014-0).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Demonstration of the configurations studied for the system composed of two potassium ions and a graphene quantum dot (
G Q D
). The atoms were positioned equidistant from the
G Q D
in all cases. (a) Configuration seen in the XY plane; (b), (c), and (d) Side view configuration with a separation of 3, 4, and 6 Å between potassium ions and the graphene sheet, respectively.

Electronic distribution before (
H O M O
and
H O M O + 1
) and after (
L U M O + 3
) the electronic transition involving
G Q D
and potassium ions. In this case, the potassium ions are positioned 3.5 Å away from the
G Q D
molecule. In gray, the carbon atoms are represented; In lilac, the potassium ions are represented; In blue and yellow, the molecular/atomic orbitals for the same electronic state are shown.

Electronic distribution before (
H O M O
and
H O M O + 1
) and after (
L U M O + 1
) the electronic transition involving
G Q D
and potassium ions. In this case, the potassium ions are positioned 4.0 Å away from the
G Q D
molecule. In gray, the carbon atoms are represented; In lilac, the potassium ions are represented; In blue and yellow, the molecular/atomic orbitals for the same electronic state are shown.

Energy levels involving the
H O M O − L U M O
orbitals of the pure
G Q D
and the
G Q D
in the presence of potassium ions. In the first column, we consider the pure
G Q D
system, where
Δ
1
indicates the difference in energy between the
H O M O − L U M O
orbitals. In the second column, we consider the complete system
K
+
@ G Q D @
K
+
, positioned at 4.0 Å. In this last case,
Δ
2
and
Δ
3
indicate the energy difference between the
H O M O − L U M O
orbitals of the
G Q D
and the
K
+
− G Q D
system, respectively.

head.ce:title: DFT-based investigation of graphene-mediated manipulation for efficient potassium ion entanglement

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto de Física, Universidade Federal de Goiás, 74.690-900, Goiânia, Goiás, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Instituto de Física, Universidade Federal de Goiás

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instituto de Física, Universidade Federal de Goiás, 74.690-900, Goiânia, Goiás, Brazil.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this paper we address the potential entanglement of potassium ions mediated by interaction with a graphene sheet using DFT. The system consists of a neutral graphene sheet (
G Q D
) positioned equidistant between two positively charged potassium ions (
K
+
). The entanglement is strongly influenced by the
K
+
@ G Q D @
K
+
separation distance, in which it is possible to observe specific electronic transitions originating exclusively from the GQD to the potassium ions, enabling the acquisition of entangled states between the two ions with fidelity close to unity, demonstrating the potential of the present system for a robust generation of quantum entanglement between alkali metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum entanglement is a fundamental phenomenon in quantum mechanics where two or more particles become intrinsically correlated in such a way that the quantum state of one particle cannot be fully described independently of the state of the others, even when they are separated by significant distances [1–3]. Indeed, this entanglement is established through quantum interactions and persists over long distances. While it challenges classical intuition, quantum entanglement is a distinctive feature of the quantum world. It finds practical applications in quantum computing [4], quantum communication for secure encryption [5], precision metrology [6], quantum teleportation [7], quantum teleportation without Bell-state measurement [8–10], etc., which enables advances in various quantum technologies.

Numerous advancements have been accomplished in the field of quantum entanglement generation, encompassing both theoretical refinements [11–14] and rigorous experimental implementations [15–19]. These developments include the formulation of novel entanglement-generation protocols, the design of sophisticated entangled states, the exploration of entanglement in multipartite systems, and the investigation of quantum entanglement across varying physical platforms, such as photonic systems, trapped ions, and superconducting qubits. Additionally, progress has been achieved in addressing issues related to the robustness, scalability, and entanglement distribution, further advancing the practical utilization of quantum entanglement for quantum communication, quantum computing, and foundational tests of quantum mechanics [3].

On the other hand, Graphene Quantum Dots (
G Q D
) represent cost-effective experimental platforms with versatile practical applications, thus making them highly attractive within the scientific research domain [20,21]. Indeed,
G Q D
s are two-dimensional nanostructures derived from graphene, composed of a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice [22,23]. They possess nanoscale dimensions and exhibit unique quantum properties, including a tunable bandgap, discrete electronic states, and quantum light emission.
G Q D
s find diverse applications across electronics, semiconductor devices, sensing, optoelectronics, energy storage, theranostics, and composite materials. They serve as building blocks for nanoelectronics, high-sensitivity sensors, efficient optoelectronic devices, and materials enhancers, making them a versatile and highly promising field in nanotechnology and materials science [24]. In this sense, we proposed recently a method for robust entanglement generation in lithium ions mediated by the interaction with a
G Q D
[25]. We verified by numerical investigations that this novel platform is capable of generating resilient entanglement within a metallic-organic system. To this end, we have employed a comprehensive electronic structure analysis using Density Functional Theory (DFT) and observed that strategically positioning lithium ions in close proximity to opposite faces of a graphene sheet ensures that the dipole–dipole interaction during the
G Q D →
Li
+
transition results in a robust entangled state for the electronic configuration of the two lithium ions.

In this work, inspired by our previous result [25], we present a study aimed at tuning the generation of quantum entanglement between two potassium ions (
K
+
) through the mediation of a
G Q D
. Our approach involves employing DFT for theoretical computation of electronic transitions. Particular emphasis will be placed on identifying transitions that manifest simultaneously and indistinguishably for both
K
+
ions. The subsequent sections of this work will be organized as follows.

In our simulations, we considered the
G Q D
sheet and the two potassium ions distributed equidistantly from the
G Q D
, as depicted in Fig. 1. When representing the charge state of the entire system, we initially have the configuration given by
|
ψ
i n
〉
G 12
=
| 0 , + , + 〉
where the subscript
G 12
represents, respectively, the charge state of the
G Q D
and the two potassium ions (here numbered as 1 and 2, without loss of generality). The quantum state
| 0 〉
indicates that the subsystem is in a neutral total charge configuration, while the state
| + 〉
indicates the presence of a positive charge in the subsystem. The favorable electronic transitions for the purpose of this work must preserve the condition that the electrons originate from the
G Q D
, which serves as an electron reservoir of the system. The destination of this excited electron from the
G Q D
is the system composed of the two potassium ions (more specifically, the
4 s
unoccupied orbitals of each ion). Despite being specific, these transitions can be obtained through optical stimulation with well-defined frequencies. In this case, after a time instant t , we can rewrite the state of the system as follows:
(1)
|
ψ
o u t
〉
G 12
=
c
1
| 0 + + 〉
G 12
+
c
2
| + 0 + 〉
G 12
+
c
3
| + + 0 〉
G 12
,
where
c
i
( i = 1 , 2 , or 3 )
represent time-dependent coefficients. In the model, grounding the
G Q D
is crucial, as the measurement cannot be directly performed on the potassium ions and serves the purpose of detecting the precise moment of the electronic transition. In this case, at the instant of the electronic transition, we will have the
G Q D
in the state
| + 〉
G
, implying that the output state (1) will collapse to:
(2)
|
ψ
o u t
′
〉
12
= α
| 0 + 〉
12
+ β
| + 0 〉
12
,
with α and β being constant coefficients. Since the introduction of a new electron to the
G Q D
via grounding, there will also be an impossibility of a electronic decay of the potassium
→ G Q D
type or even a second
G Q D →
potassium excitation. We emphasize that the target state for generation in this system is the maximally entangled state, given by
| ϕ 〉
12
=
1
2
| 0 + 〉
12
+
e
i φ
| + 0 〉
12
, with φ being an arbitrary phase.

The results presented in this work were obtained using DFT [26,27], in conjunction with the exchange–correlation functional CAM-B3LYP [28], a triple- ζ basis set proposed by Pople with polarization functions described by 6-311G(d,p) [29], and also the empirical dispersion model GD3 (Grimme-D3) [30] for all electronic structure calculations. Thus, we have added a correction that takes into account London dispersion interactions, which can be important in molecular systems. In this case, the GD3 model is known to be more accurate in a variety of chemical systems and is widely used in computational chemistry calculations to obtain more precise results in systems that can receive a fundamental contribution involving dispersion interactions, especially in systems where electronic transitions are the focus.

The system composed of a
G Q D
and two potassium ions (
K
+
) was structured, keeping the distances between
G Q D
and
K
+
ions constant from
d = 3 . 0
Å to 6.0 Å, varying in 0.5 Å increments. All configurations were optimized and subsequently subjected to electronic transition calculations to obtain the first 30 excited states in each system. In each case, the electronic transitions obtained were analyzed, focusing exclusively on those electronic transitions that involve an electron exchange between the neutral
G Q D
and potassium ions. In this case, the target transition should map a charge transfer from the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (
H O M O
) of the
G Q D
to the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (
L U M O
) of the potassium ions, specifically the unoccupied
4 s
orbital in these ions. It is crucial that the electronic transitions clearly do not involve degeneracy in the
4 s
orbitals of the potassium ions, preserving the final non-localization of the electron on a specific potassium ion. Therefore, electronic transitions that map molecular orbitals outside of this
H O M
O
G Q D
to
4
s
K
1
+
and
K
2
+
condition are not the focus of this application and analysis. It is also worth noting that electronic transitions with zero oscillator strength ( f ) were excluded. All quantum calculations were performed using the Gaussian program, version 16 [31].

In this discussion, our focus will be on presenting only the results that support the objective of this study. Therefore, configurations that do not exhibit transitions with the specified characteristics will not be discussed and/or presented. For the configuration with a
G Q D −
K
+
distance of 3.5 Å, we observed one possible electronic transition involving two different molecular orbitals localized in the
G Q D
molecule (
H O M O
and
H O M O − 1
) and the potassium ions (
4 s
orbitals) located as
L U M O + 3
in the
K
+
@
G Q D
@
K
+
configuration, as can be seen in Fig. 2. Each transition (
H O M O
→
L U M O + 3
and
H O M O − 1
→
L U M O + 3
) exhibits coefficients with values equal to
α =
e
i φ
β = 0 . 69938
. These electronic transitions occur at an energy of approximately
3 . 4771
eV
(corresponding to a wavelength of
λ = 356 . 6
nm
) with an oscillator strength
f = 0 . 0046
. The obtained result does not distinguish the location of the electron involved after the electronic transition, indicating that the final states describing the charged and uncharged potassium ions are in a superposition state, as expected and described by Eq. (2).

The fidelity of the state produced after the electronic transition is given by
F =
|
〈 ϕ |
ψ
o u t
′
〉
|
2
=
(
| α |
2
+
| β |
2
+ 2 ℜ
{ α
β
∗
}
)
/ 2
. Here,
ℜ
{ O }
represents the real part of the argument O , and ∗ indicates the complex conjugate of the term. Therefore, considering the results of the TD-DFT calculations that describe the event produced in the transitions, we can obtain the fidelity F of the generated state as approximately 97.8%. The 2.2% difference from the state we intended to generate may be related to the numerical accuracy of the TD-DFT calculations.

For a distance of 4.0 Å between the elements that make up the system, once again, we observe an electronic transition that characterizes the entanglement of the potassium ions. This electronic transition involves two different molecular orbitals located on the
G Q D
, specifically located as
H O M O
and
H O M O − 1
in the
K
+
@ G Q D @
K
+
system. In these transitions, the atomic orbitals involved are the
4 s
orbitals located on the two potassium ions, now positioned as the
L U M O + 1
orbital in the
K
+
@ G Q D @
K
+
system. These electronic transitions (
H O M O → L U M O + 1
and
H O M O − 1 → L U M O + 1
) have coefficients equal to
α =
e
i φ
β = 0 . 70024
and occur at an energy of approximately
3 . 127
eV
(corresponding to a wavelength of
λ = 396 . 4
nm
) with an oscillator strength
f = 0 . 0006
. Note that the state accessed by the electron has a lower energy difference than that obtained in the previous configuration (i.e., 3.5 Å). The fidelity F of the generated state is slightly higher than that obtained previously, being approximately 98.1%. In Fig. 3, we show the electronic distributions before and after the transition.

For the separated system with a distance of 4.5 Å, the electronic transitions obtained are similar to those obtained for the configuration with a separation of 4.0 Å between the potassium ions and the
G Q D
. The electronic transitions occur at an energy of approximately 2.880 eV (corresponding to a wavelength of
λ = 430 . 5
nm
) with an oscillator strength of
f = 0 . 0001
. For this case, the values of the coefficients for the electronic transitions obtained are close to
α =
e
i φ
β = 0 . 69961
. As a result, the fidelity F of the generated state shows a slight decrease compared to the result obtained for the configuration with a 4.0 Å separation, now being
F = 97 . 9 %
. The electronic distributions before and after the electronic transition for the potassium ions at a distance of 4.5 Å are similar to those shown for the previous situation (see Fig. 3).

We do not observe electronic transitions that characterize the entanglement of the potassium ions in configurations with distances above the value of 4.5 Å. This demonstrates that the region in which we can produce entanglement of the charge states of the potassium ions is between
3 . 5 − 4 . 5
Å (
G Q D −
K
+
distance). However, these distances exhibit a considerable difference in the wavelength for each configuration. (
△ λ = 430 . 5 − 356 . 6 = 73 . 9
nm
) This separation in wavelength is crucial to precisely select the electronic transition involving the
G Q D
orbitals and the potassium ion orbitals, favoring the organic-metallic transition necessary for the entanglement of the system composed of the two potassium ions.

To conclude our analysis, we examined the behavior of the energy level distributions of the orbitals in systems composed of only the
G Q D
compared to those considering the potassium ions as well. As shown in Fig. 4 we can observe that the energies of the molecular orbitals of the
G Q D
increase (in absolute value) when the potassium ions are added to the system. Indeed, the energy differences between the
H O M O − L U M O
orbitals of the isolated
G Q D
are approximately
Δ
1
= 4 . 69
eV
, while for the system containing the potassium ions, we have an energy gap change in the
G Q D
of
Δ
2
= 4 . 659
eV and
Δ
3
= 4 . 393
eV
when the atomic orbitals of the potassium ions are involved (for the example with a distance of 4.0 Å). This positioning of the
4 s
orbitals of the potassium ions between the
H O M O − L U M O
orbitals of the
G Q D
favors the organic-metallic electronic transition, which in the architecture of the proposed system can act as a tuner for the generation of entanglement of the involved potassium ions.

In conclusion, we conducted a precise investigation to modulate the generation of quantum entanglement between two potassium ions (
K
+
), utilizing a
G Q D
as a mediator, with DFT calculations, providing fundamental insights. Indeed, a critical discovery emerged: a clear observation that we did not detect electronic transitions indicative of entanglement between the potassium ions when they were separated by distances exceeding
4 . 5 Å
(the
G Q D −
K
+
distance). This observation highlighted a specific and spatial range in the interaction between potassium ions and the
G Q D
within which we could induce the entanglement of the charge states of potassium ions, limited to the
3 . 5 − 4 . 5 Å
interval.

Furthermore, within this narrow distance range, we observed significant differences in wavelength ( △
λ = 73 . 9
nm
) for electronic transitions in each configuration. This difference is essential, as it allows us to precisely select electronic transitions between the orbitals of the
G Q D
and potassium ions. Additionally, the strategic insertion of the previously unoccupied
4 s
orbitals of the potassium ions between the
H O M O − L U M O
orbitals of the
G Q D
plays a fundamental role in the organic-metallic electronic transition required for the modulation of the desired entanglement. This unique arrangement, existing only within the narrow
G Q D −
K
+
distance range, promotes electronic transitions and serves as a fine-tuning mechanism within the proposed system, enabling the controlled generation of entanglement between the potassium ions. These findings significantly advance our understanding of controlled quantum entanglement in such a system and carry substantial implications for potential applications in quantum technology.

Guilherme Colherinhas: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wesley B. Cardoso: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support of the Brazilian agencies CNPq (WBC: #306105/2022-5; GC: #304903/2020-5) and CAPES. This work was also performed as part of the Brazilian National Institute of Science and Technology (INCT) for Quantum Information (#465469/2014-0).
